text: The surgeon mobilizes the ascending colon, preparing to make a colotomy near the tattooed area marking the location of the submucosal lipoma.

The surgeon carefully separates the omentum from the hepatic flexure, visually identifying the tattoo marking the location of the submucosal lipoma. The fullness of the tissue is visible, indicating the presence of the lipoma.

The surgeon makes an incision on the colon, ensuring not to compromise blood flow or narrow the lumen. The port placement is similar to that used in a right hemicolectomy. The patient had undergone both mechanical and antibiotic bowel preparation.

The surgeon orients the surgical field to ensure the incision does not narrow the lumen of the colon. The colotomy is made, and the surgeon visually searches for the submucosal lipoma, considering its location relative to the incision.

The surgeon identifies the lipoma on the posterior wall of the colon, lifting it for excision. A white load stapler is used to staple the lipoma, similar to the technique used in a right hemicolectomy.

The surgeon excises the specimen and places it in the right upper quadrant. Two silk sutures are applied at both ends of the incision to aid in retraction and to mark the ends of the colotomy for closure.
The surgeon passes a Maryland dissector to ensure the distal lumen remains open, marking the proximal end of the incision to assist with retraction if necessary.
The surgeon sutures the incision in two layers, completing the closure of the surgical site.
